title: Implement Resource-Based Authorization for ShopFlow
description: Build resource-based authorization using IAuthorizationService to enforce ownership and access control for specific resources like orders, products, and user profiles in ShopFlow.
instructions:
Implement resource-based authorization where access depends on ownership of specific resources, not just roles or claims. Users should only modify their own data.

**Requirements:**

1. **Create Resource Models with Ownership:**
   - Order class with OwnerId property
   - Product class with SellerId property
   - UserProfile class with UserId property

2. **Implement Custom Authorization Handler:**
   - ResourceOwnerAuthorizationHandler that checks if current user owns the resource
   - Support different operations: Read, Update, Delete
   - Admin users can access any resource (override ownership)

3. **Create Resource-Based Requirements:**
   - ResourceOwnerRequirement with ResourceType and Operation parameters

4. **Create Protected Endpoints with Resource Auth:**
   - GET /api/orders/{id} - Owner or Admin can view
   - PUT /api/orders/{id} - Only owner can update
   - DELETE /api/orders/{id} - Only owner or Admin can delete
   - PUT /api/products/{id} - Only seller (product owner) can update
   - GET /api/users/{id}/profile - Only the user or Admin can view

5. **Use IAuthorizationService for Imperative Checks:**
   - Inject IAuthorizationService into endpoints
   - Call AuthorizeAsync with the resource and requirement

**Scenarios to Support:**
- User A can view, update, delete their own orders
- User A cannot access User B's orders (returns 403)
- Admin can view/update/delete any order (full override)
- Product sellers can only update their own products
- Users can only view their own profile, not others'

**Hint:** Resource-based auth uses IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(user, resource, policyName) - pass the actual resource object so handlers can check ownership.

testCases.description: Should have AuthorizeAsync call with resource

hints.text: Inject IAuthorizationService into your endpoint handler to perform resource-based checks.
Use await authorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(User, resource, "PolicyName") to check access.
Create an IAuthorizationRequirement that accepts the resource type and operation type.
In your handler, check if context.Resource is the expected type and cast it.
Always allow Admin role to bypass ownership checks - check context.User.IsInRole("Admin") first.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not passing the actual resource to AuthorizeAsync
commonMistakes.consequence: The authorization handler receives null resource and cannot check ownership.
commonMistakes.correction: Pass the loaded entity: await authService.AuthorizeAsync(User, order, "CanViewOrder")

commonMistakes.mistake: Checking resource ownership before verifying authentication
commonMistakes.consequence: Anonymous users might cause null reference exceptions.
commonMistakes.correction: Check context.User.Identity?.IsAuthenticated first, or use [Authorize] attribute on endpoint.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not handling the case where resource doesn't exist
commonMistakes.consequence: Returns 403 when should return 404 - user doesn't know if resource exists or they lack access.
commonMistakes.correction: Check if resource exists first (return 404), then check authorization (return 403).

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to register the authorization handler
commonMistakes.consequence: Policy evaluation fails because the handler isn't in DI container.
